metadata.title: Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record of What the Cell Encountered

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: It amazes me that BPDE attacks guanine at specific trinucleotide motifs and locks in G-to-T transversions that survive as a fingerprint for decades. I want to understand how analysts read these signatures backward. Can you (1) explain the 96-channel trinucleotide framework used to define a mutational signature, (2) walk through how the aflatoxin spike lands so sharply at TP53 codon 249 rather than spreading out, and (3) tell me whether two carcinogens can produce overlapping signatures that are impossible to disentangle?

props.command: It amazes me that BPDE attacks guanine at specific trinucleotide motifs and locks in G-to-T transversions that survive as a fingerprint for decades. I want to understand how analysts read these signatures backward. Can you (1) explain the 96-channel trinucleotide framework used to define a mutational signature, (2) walk through how the aflatoxin spike lands so sharply at TP53 codon 249 rather than spreading out, and (3) tell me whether two carcinogens can produce overlapping signatures that are impossible to disentangle?
props.segment: Why the Tumor Genome Is a

beats.narration_text: A sequencing report lands in 2025. It accurately names what was inside a lung cell in 1985.
beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record

beats.narration_text: An oncologist reads: 340 mutations, dominant pattern G-to-T transversions at specific trinucleotide contexts. She recognizes it before reading the clinical history.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] sequencing report panel with G->T dominant pattern, Signature 4 chip

beats.narration_text: Each carcinogen leaves a distinctive mutation pattern that persists for decades. Why? How does a chemical write a permanent fingerprint into DNA?
beats.new_visual_element: [QUESTION] gap formula on screen: pattern persists decades -- why?

beats.narration_text: Mutations are heritable changes in the DNA sequence. When a cell divides, every daughter cell inherits exactly the same altered letter.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] cell division tree: G->T mutation inherited across generations

beats.narration_text: A chemical hits one cell once. You might expect one random mutation -- lost in millions of normal letters, invisible in the noise.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] genome grid: one crimson G among teal letters

beats.narration_text: But benzo-a-pyrene doesn't hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE -- which attacks guanine specifically, at specific trinucleotide motifs.

props.headline: But benzo-a-pyrene doesn't hit randomly. Enzymes convert it to BPDE --…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] benzo[a]pyrene -> BPDE -> guanine adduct -> G->T chain

beats.narration_text: The BPDE adduct distorts the helix. DNA polymerase can't read through it accurately. It inserts adenine opposite the damaged guanine.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] DNA ladder: adduct on G, kinked rung, polymerase inserts A (wrong)

beats.narration_text: After one more replication round, that mismatch becomes a fixed G-to-T transversion -- permanent, heritable, copied into every daughter cell forever.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two daughter cells: one G:C (teal), one T:A (crimson, fixed mutation)

beats.narration_text: UV works differently. A photon fuses adjacent pyrimidines into a cyclobutane dimer. Polymerase inserts adenine opposite both -- leaving C-to-T transitions at dipyrimidine sites.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] UV dimer formation: C-C + photon -> C=C dimer -> C->T transition

beats.narration_text: Decades of exposure means thousands of these context-specific errors. The pattern accumulates. The chemical fingerprint grows stronger with every pack-year, every hour of sun.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] bar chart growing: G->T bars at GpC context rising over time

beats.narration_text: Four carcinogens, four fingerprints. Tobacco: G-to-T. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin: one sharp spike at TP53 codon 249. MMR deficiency: hundreds of mutations, evenly spread.

props.headline: Four carcinogens, four fingerprints. Tobacco: G-to-T. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin:…

beats.new_visual_element: [STILL geo] four mutational signature bar charts side-by-side

beats.narration_text: A lung cancer in a never-smoker with the tobacco signature reveals secondary exposure the clinical history missed. The genome is a more reliable witness than memory.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] two-column: genome says tobacco signature / file says never-smoker

beats.narration_text: A student reads four tumor genomes. Each came from a different patient. The patterns will name the exposure before the clinical file is opened.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four empty genome boxes -- illustrative example setup

beats.narration_text: Melanoma: C-to-T, UV. Lung adenocarcinoma: G-to-T, tobacco. Liver tumor: codon 249 spike, aflatoxin. Colon cancer: 400 mutations, uniform -- MMR deficiency. Four histories. Illustrative.
beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] four illustrated tumor genome cards with pattern and carcinogen labels (illustrative)

beats.narration_text: Each carcinogen makes adducts at specific sequence contexts. Polymerase misreads them with context-specific bias. The pattern is fixed, heritable -- a chemical fingerprint in DNA.
beats.new_visual_element: [ENDCARD] CANCER BIOLOGY eyebrow, recap: fingerprint in DNA

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Four carcinogens, four fingerprints. Tobacco: G-to-T. UV: C-to-T at dipyrimidines. Aflatoxin: one sharp spike at TP53 codon 249. MMR deficiency: hundreds of mutations, evenly spread. A lung cancer in a never-smoker with the tobacco signature reveals secondary exposure the clinical history missed. The genome is a more reliable witness than memory.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Why the Tumor Genome Is a Fossil Record of What the Cell Encountered] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
